attribution.text: Route, junction, and history details adapted from the Wikipedia article "Volcanic Legacy Scenic Byway", by Wikipedia contributors, licensed under CC BY-SA 4.0.

sources.name: Federal Highway Administration — National Scenic Byways

paragraphs: The Volcanic Legacy Scenic Byway is officially divided into 11 regions, with the first four regions forming the Lassen Scenic Byway. There is also an alternate route through Medicine Lake.

The bypass begins at the intersection of SR 44 and SR 89 at the northern boundary of Lassen Volcanic National Park. It then runs south along SR 89 through the park, passing by Manzanita Lake and through an unnamed pass between Lassen Peak and Bumpass Mountain. After exiting the park, the bypass and SR 89 join with SR 36 east of Mineral (the segment of SR 36 through Mineral is not officially part of the byway). The highways then head east to Chester on the northwest side of Lake Almanor.

The bypass then runs along the highways looping around Lake Almanor, consisting of: SR 36 running along the north side of the lake between Chester and Westwood, SR 147 on the lake's eastern side between Westwood and Canyondam, and SR 89 from Canyondam back to Chester.
From Chester, SR 36 and the byway continues east to its intersection with SR 44 west of Susanville (the segment of SR 36 between SR 44 and Susanville is not officially part of the byway).

references.text: "The Volcanic Legacy Scenic Byway All-American Road". Yreka, California: Volcanic Legacy Community Partnership. 2020. Retrieved June 1, 2024.
"Regions Along the Byway". volcaniclegacybyway.org/. Volcanic Legacy Community Partnership. Retrieved September 1, 2025.
"Chester to Susanville". volcaniclegacybyway.org/. Volcanic Legacy Community Partnership. Retrieved September 1, 2025.

A Cascade of Volcanoes from Rim Drive Overlook
gallery.desc: California's Mount Shasta can barely be seen on the horizon from an overlook on Crater Lake National Park's Rim Drive. Other volcanic peaks and cones in the Cascade Range are more visible. Location: Crater Lake National Park, Oregon (42.940° N 122.179° W) Status: Public domain. Photo by A. E. Crane
gallery.credit: A. E. Crane, U.S. Department of Transportation · Public domain

gallery.cap: Applegate Trail Ferry Crossing at the Klamath River
gallery.desc: The Topsy Bridge now crosses the Klamath River west of Keno, Oregon, near where settlers once crossed on the Applegate Trail. Location: Klamath River Recreation Site, Oregon (42.136° N 122.030° W) Status: Public domain. Photo by A. E. Crane
gallery.credit: A. E. Crane, U.S. Department of Transportation · Public domain

gallery.desc: People at the Watchman Overlook stand behind a guard rail and look to the lake below. Above them, the jagged lava teeth of the Devil's Backbone formation jab into the sky along the edge of Crater Lake. Location: Crater Lake National Park, Oregon (42.946° N 122.169° W) Status: Public domain. Photo by A. E. Crane
gallery.credit: A. E. Crane, U.S. Department of Transportation · Public domain

gallery.desc: Tall grasses and other wetland plants line the open waters of Discovery Marsh just across the road from the Klamath Basin National Wildlife Refuges Visitor Center on Hill Road. Location: Tulelake, California (41.934° N 121.564° W) Status: Public domain. Photo by A. E. Crane

gallery.cap: Driving OR 140 past Upper Klamath Lake
gallery.desc: Trees and shoreline shrubs decorate the landscape along Oregon State Highway 140 past Upper Klamath Lake. Location: OR-140, Oregon (42.300° N 121.934° W) Status: Public domain. Photo by A. E. Crane
gallery.credit: A. E. Crane, U.S. Department of Transportation · Public domain

gallery.desc: Intrinsic qualities along the byway can continue to be preserved and enhanced through stewardship and education. Providing children with knowledge about conservation will help them understand the importance and true value of our resources. Location: (42.868° N 122.168° W) Status: Public domain. Dave Menke and United States Fish and Wildlife Service, 2001

A ridge made of an ancient lava flow rises behind a set of signs directing travelers on the Volcanic Legacy Scenic Byway to Lava Bed National Monument and the Klamath Basin National Wildlife Refuges (namely, its visitor center.) The post also includes an America's Byways roadsign. Location: Tulelake, California (41.953° N 121.566° W) Status: Public domain. Photo by A. E. Crane
gallery.credit: A. E. Crane, U.S. Department of Transportation · Public domain

gallery.cap: General Store in Downtown Fort Klamath
gallery.desc: The Fort Klamath General Store stands next to the highway, offering groceries, snacks and supplies to people traveling to and from Crater Lake National Park. Location: Fort Klamath, Oregon (42.701° N 121.996° W) Status: Public domain. Photo by A. E. Crane
gallery.credit: A. E. Crane, U.S. Department of Transportation · Public domain

gallery.desc: Managed agriculture and timber lands are important attributes of the byway, offering travelers glimpes into the rural lifestyle and culture of the region. The Volcanic Legacy Scenic Byway is a place where the ever-changing worlds of the past and present unite in harmony. Location: (40.831° N 121.513° W) Status: Public domain. Siskiyou County Visitors Bureau, 2001

Jumbled black lava forms the aptly-named "Devil's Homestead" section of Lava Beds National Monument. Location: Lava Beds National Monument, California (41.793° N 121.562° W) Status: Public domain. Photo by A. E. Crane
gallery.credit: A. E. Crane, U.S. Department of Transportation · Public domain

gallery.desc: A row of motorcycles await their owners at one of the overlooks on the Crater Rim Drive. Location: Crater Lake National Park, Oregon (42.940° N 122.180° W) Status: Public domain. Photo by A. E. Crane

Mount Shasta is visible from hundreds of miles away, appearing like an island in the sky. The sheer vastness of Mount Shasta along the byway is enough to capture the traveler's attention for awhile, but this is only one of the many experiences the byway offers. Location: (41.406° N 122.196° W) Status: Public domain. Caia Cupito and Ore-Cal RC D, 2001

An interpretive panel at the Vanished Volcano roadside pullout on SR 62 shows the extent of the Mt. Mazama shield volcano before the massive eruption that eventually formed Crater Lake. Location: OR 62, Klamath County, Oregon (42.734° N 122.017° W) Status: Public domain. Photo by A. E. Crane
gallery.credit: A. E. Crane, U.S. Department of Transportation · Public domain
